sn4.13:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Rājagaha in the Maddakucchi Deer Reserve.
sn4.13:1.3: Now at that time his foot had been pierced by a stone sliver.
sn4.13:1.4: Excruciating were the bodily feelings that developed within him—painful, fierce, sharp, wracking, repellent, disagreeable—
sn4.13:1.5: but he endured them mindful, alert, & unperturbed.
sn4.13:1.6: Having had his outer robe folded in four and laid out, he lay down on his right side in the lion’s posture—with one foot placed on top of the other—mindful & alert.
sn4.13:1.7: Then Māra the Evil One went to the Blessed One and recited this verse in his presence:
sn4.13:2.1: “Are you lying there in a stupor, or drunk on poetry?
sn4.13:2.2: Are your goals so very few?
sn4.13:2.3: All alone in a secluded lodging,
sn4.13:2.4: what is this dreamer, this sleepy-face?”
sn4.13:3.1: The Buddha: “I lie here, not in a stupor, nor drunk on poetry.
sn4.13:3.2: My goal attained, I am sorrow-free.
sn4.13:3.3: All alone in a secluded lodging, I lie down with sympathy
sn4.13:4.1: Even those pierced in the chest with an arrow,
sn4.13:4.3: even they with their arrows are able to sleep.
sn4.13:4.4: So why shouldn’t I, with my arrow removed?
sn4.13:5.1: I’m not awake with worry, nor afraid to sleep.
sn4.13:5.3: I see no threat of decline in any world at all.
sn4.13:5.4: That’s why I sleep with sympathy for all beings.”
sn4.13:6.1: Then Māra the Evil One—sad & dejected at realizing, “The Blessed One knows me; the One Well-Gone knows me”—vanished right there.
